skill.name: commit-helper
skill.description: Intelligent commit message generation following conventional commit format.
skill.summary: Intelligent commit message generation following conventional commit format.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution and flagged weak cryptography where no cryptographic code exists. The only confirmed behavior is guidance to run read-only git commands for staged diffs and recent commit history.

low_findings.title: Read-only Git Command Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill asks the agent to inspect staged changes with git diff --staged and recent history with git log --oneline -10. This is legitimate for commit generation, but it requires repository command execution and may expose local code context to the model.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly names two git commands and both are read-only. There is no evidence of shell interpolation, persistence, network access, or destructive command execution.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick and Cryptography False Positives
low_findings.description: Most static findings are Markdown inline code, code fences, commit examples, or conventional commit labels. The weak cryptography findings at the cited lines do not correspond to hashing, encryption, or security-sensitive code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown documentation and examples, not executable Ruby or cryptographic implementation. No evidence found for malware, data exfiltration, or prompt injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill depends on git command output to create commit messages. The commands are read-only and appropriate for the skill purpose, but external command execution should remain visible to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command names are explicit and semantically necessary for the workflow. The risk is limited because the commands only inspect local git state.

content.value_statement: Developers often lose time turning code changes into clear commit messages. This skill reviews staged git changes and drafts concise Conventional Commit messages.
actual_capabilities: Reviews staged git changes with git diff --staged.
Checks recent commit history to match local project style.
Suggests Conventional Commit types such as feat, fix, docs, test, and chore.
Adds optional scopes when a changed component is clear.
Drafts subject, body, and footer text for commit messages.
limitations: It only analyzes changes that are already staged.
It does not create commits or modify repository files by itself.
It relies on local git command output being available.
It cannot infer issue numbers unless they appear in the context.

use_cases.title: Prepare clean feature commits
use_cases.description: Generate a clear feat commit after staging a finished product change.

use_cases.title: Standardize team commit style
use_cases.description: Match the tone and format of recent commits before submitting work.

use_cases.title: Write concise maintenance commits
use_cases.description: Turn dependency, test, documentation, or cleanup changes into accurate chore, test, or docs messages.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic commit message
prompt_templates.prompt: Use commit-helper to review my staged changes and suggest one Conventional Commit message.

prompt_templates.title: Add a specific scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Use commit-helper with the api scope and create a concise commit message for my staged changes.

prompt_templates.title: Force a commit type
prompt_templates.prompt: Use commit-helper with type fix and explain why that type fits the staged changes.

prompt_templates.title: Match repository history
prompt_templates.prompt: Use commit-helper to inspect staged changes and recent commit history, then draft the best commit subject and body.

output_examples.input: Staged files add password reset endpoints and user interface flow.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: feat(auth): add password reset flow. The body explains the new endpoint and user-facing recovery path.

output_examples.input: Staged files update tests for a null email validation bug.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: fix(validation): handle null email values. The body notes the previous failure and expected behavior.

output_examples.input: Staged files update README examples for version two API routes.
output_examples.output: Suggested message: docs: update API v2 examples. No body is needed because the documentation change is narrow.

best_practices: Stage only related changes before invoking the skill.
Review the suggested type and scope before committing.
Add issue references when the staged change closes tracked work.
anti_patterns: Do not use it on broad mixed changes without separating commits.
Do not accept a message that describes files instead of behavior.
Do not include private secrets or credentials in staged changes.

faq.question: Does this skill create the git commit?
faq.answer: No. It drafts a commit message for review. The user remains responsible for running git commit.

faq.question: Does it require staged changes?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow is based on git diff --staged, so unstaged changes are outside its normal review.

faq.question: Can it follow my project style?
faq.answer: Yes. It checks recent commit history and uses that context when drafting the message.

faq.question: Can I request a specific commit type?
faq.answer: Yes. The usage examples include type overrides such as fix, and scopes can also be requested.

faq.question: Is this safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: It uses local read-only git commands. Users should still avoid staging secrets or highly sensitive content.

faq.question: Does it support breaking changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It can include a BREAKING CHANGE footer when the staged changes justify one.
